tests.title: IELTS Academic Reading — Test 03
tests.description: Original full-length Academic Reading test: 3 passages, 40 questions, 60 minutes.

sections.title: Passage 1 — Why Handmade Survives
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 1–13.
sections.passage:
Why Handmade Survives

¶1 When the factories of the industrial revolution began turning out goods by the thousand, many observers assumed that handmade objects were doomed. Why would anyone pay a craftsman to make a single chair slowly when a machine could produce a hundred near-identical ones in a day, at a fraction of the cost? For a time the prediction seemed to be coming true, as workshops closed and ancient trades dwindled. And yet, more than two centuries later, the handmade has stubbornly refused to disappear. In wealthy and developing countries alike, craftspeople still throw pots, weave cloth, forge metal and carve wood, and in a good many places the demand for their work is now growing rather than shrinking. The reasons for this survival reveal a great deal about what people truly value in the things they choose to own.

¶2 Part of the answer lies in what machines simply cannot do. Mass production depends on uniformity: every item that leaves the line is meant to be identical to the last, and any variation is treated as a defect to be eliminated. A handmade object is the very opposite. The slight unevenness of a thrown bowl, the irregular grain left by a chisel, the small asymmetries that betray the presence of a human hand, are not flaws but the very source of its appeal. Each piece is, in a literal sense, unrepeatable. To own one is to own something that exists nowhere else on earth, and that quality has only grown more precious as the world has filled up with the perfectly uniform and the endlessly duplicated.

¶3 Craft is also a store of knowledge, and a peculiarly fragile one. The skill of a master potter or weaver is rarely written down; it lives in the hands, accumulated over decades and passed on by demonstration and imitation rather than by instruction. When such a person dies without an apprentice, a body of understanding built up over a whole lifetime can vanish in an afternoon, and techniques that took generations to perfect may be lost beyond recovery. Several traditional crafts have come within a single death of extinction, kept alive only because a younger maker sought out the last practitioner in time. This dependence on living memory makes craft uniquely vulnerable, and it explains why its champions often speak of it in the language usually reserved for endangered species.

¶4 There is, too, a hard commercial logic at work. Unable to compete with factories on price, the surviving crafts have moved upmarket, trading on exactly those qualities that machines cannot offer: individuality, authenticity and a story. A handwoven rug or a hand-thrown mug commands a premium precisely because it is not mass-produced, and buyers are frequently willing to pay for the sense of connection to a real person and a real place. In many regions craft has become entangled with tourism, as visitors seek out workshops and carry home objects that feel rooted in a particular culture. Work that was once merely ordinary has been quietly reframed as something much closer to art.

¶5 Beyond economics lies a question of meaning. Psychologists who study work have noted the deep satisfaction that comes from making a whole object with one's own hands, from beginning to end, in contrast to the narrow, fragmented tasks of much modern employment. For the maker, craft offers a sense of competence and completion that has become increasingly rare. For the buyer, a handmade possession can feel like a quiet protest against a throwaway culture in which so much is designed from the outset to be discarded. To choose the slow, durable and repairable over the fast, cheap and disposable is, for many people, a small but genuine statement about how they wish to live.

¶6 Modern technology, so often blamed for the decline of craft, has unexpectedly come to its rescue. Online marketplaces allow a weaver in a remote village to sell directly to customers on the other side of the world, bypassing the middlemen who once swallowed most of the profit. Social media lets makers show their process, build a loyal following and command better prices, while video tutorials spread once-secret techniques to anyone willing to learn them. The very tools of the digital age, it turns out, can sustain the most analogue of activities, knitting scattered makers and buyers into communities that would have been quite impossible only a generation ago.

¶7 None of this amounts to a simple victory of old ways over new. The crafts that thrive today are rarely frozen in the past; they borrow modern materials, adapt to contemporary taste, and sit perfectly comfortably alongside the factory goods that fill most of our homes. What has really changed is a growing recognition that handmade and machine-made need not be rivals at all. Each does something the other cannot, and a culture has ample room for both. The survival of craft, in the end, is less a rejection of progress than a useful reminder that efficiency is not the only thing human beings have ever cared about.

questions.text: Early observers of industrialisation expected handmade goods to die out.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says many observers 'assumed that handmade objects were doomed', supporting the statement.

evidence.quote: handmade objects were doomed

questions.text: In handmade work, small variations are considered faults.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says such asymmetries 'are not flaws but the very source of its appeal', contradicting the statement.

evidence.quote: are not flaws but the very source of its appeal

questions.text: The skills of a master craftsman are usually written down.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says such skill 'is rarely written down; it lives in the hands', so the statement is false.

evidence.quote: is rarely written down

questions.text: Some crafts were saved only because a younger maker reached the last expert in time.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says crafts were 'kept alive only because a younger maker sought out the last practitioner in time'.

evidence.quote: a younger maker sought out the last practitioner in time

questions.text: Handmade goods are now generally cheaper than factory-made ones.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says crafts are 'Unable to compete with factories on price' and command 'a premium', so they are not cheaper.

evidence.quote: Unable to compete with factories on price

questions.text: Surviving crafts now trade on individuality, authenticity and a ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says crafts trade on 'individuality, authenticity and a story'.

evidence.quote: individuality, authenticity and a story

questions.text: Making a whole object by hand gives the maker a sense of competence and ______.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says craft offers 'a sense of competence and completion'.

evidence.quote: a sense of competence and completion

questions.text: ______ marketplaces let makers sell directly to distant customers.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 says 'Online marketplaces allow a weaver in a remote village to sell directly to customers'.

evidence.quote: Online marketplaces allow a weaver

questions.text: Which paragraph compares the loss of craft skills to the loss of endangered species?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says champions speak of craft 'in the language usually reserved for endangered species'.

evidence.quote: the language usually reserved for endangered species

questions.text: Which paragraph gives examples of digital tools helping traditional makers?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 6 describes online marketplaces and 'Social media lets makers show their process'.

evidence.quote: Social media lets makers show their process

questions.text: Which paragraph argues that handmade and machine-made goods are not rivals?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 states that 'handmade and machine-made need not be rivals at all'.

evidence.quote: handmade and machine-made need not be rivals at all

questions.text: Which paragraph describes how craft has become linked with tourism?
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4 says 'craft has become entangled with tourism'.

evidence.quote: craft has become entangled with tourism

questions.text: According to Paragraph 5, owning a handmade item can feel like a quiet ______ against throwaway culture.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says a handmade possession can 'feel like a quiet protest against a throwaway culture'.

evidence.quote: a quiet protest against a throwaway culture

sections.title: Passage 2 — The Archaeology of Rubbish
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14–26.
sections.passage:
The Archaeology of Rubbish

¶1 Archaeology is popularly associated with treasure: golden masks, marble statues and the sealed tombs of kings. Yet some of the richest sources of information about the human past are far less glamorous. They are rubbish heaps, the discarded remains of everyday life, known to archaeologists as middens. A palace tells us how a ruler wished to be remembered; a rubbish heap tells us what ordinary people actually ate, made, used and threw away. Precisely because no one ever intended it to be preserved or to carry a message, refuse offers a uniquely honest record of the past, free from the boasting and propaganda that shape monuments and official written histories alike. The grand and the deliberate survive because someone wished them to; the ordinary and the accidental survive, when they survive at all, by sheer luck, and it is often in that lucky residue that the real texture of daily life lies hidden.

¶2 What survives in a midden depends on the material and on the conditions around it. Hard substances such as bone, shell, pottery and stone endure for thousands of years, while cloth, wood and food normally rot away unless the circumstances are unusual. From the durable fragments that do remain, archaeologists can reconstruct a surprising amount: the bones reveal which animals were eaten and how they were butchered, charred seeds show which crops were grown, and broken pots indicate how food was stored and cooked. Even the shells of shellfish, piled up over many centuries, can map the shifting diet of a community and the seasons in which it gathered its food. A single well-studied heap can, in this way, yield a portrait of a vanished community's meals more detailed than any banquet that was ever recorded in writing.

¶3 A rubbish heap is also, in effect, a layered archive. Because fresh waste is dropped on top of older waste, a midden builds up in horizontal bands, the deepest of them the oldest and the highest the most recent. By digging carefully down through these layers, using a technique known as stratigraphy, archaeologists can trace change over time at a single spot, watching diets shift, new materials appear and old ones fall out of use. When this is combined with scientific methods of dating, the layering turns a heap of refuse into something close to a calendar, allowing events and changes to be placed in a reliable order. A disturbed or jumbled layer is itself informative, hinting at a flood, a rebuilding or some other interruption to the ordinary rhythm of disposal.

¶4 The insights that emerge can be genuinely surprising. Waste often reveals inequalities that official records carefully conceal: the rubbish of a wealthy household differs sharply from that of a poor one, in the cuts of meat, the quality of the pottery and the sheer variety of the goods thrown out. A sudden change within a midden can mark a moment of upheaval, such as the arrival of a new people or the collapse of a trade route that had once supplied exotic foods. In several cases rubbish has overturned assumptions drawn from grander sources, showing that what people actually consumed differed from what their leaders recorded or their laws prescribed. The gap between the official version of a society and the plain evidence of its bins is frequently the place where the most interesting history is waiting to be found.

¶5 The study of waste is by no means confined to the distant past. In the twentieth century a field sometimes called garbology turned the same methods on modern landfill, with results that unsettled some of those who had funded it. Researchers found a persistent gap between what people said they did and what their rubbish proved they had done: households reported eating less junk food, and wasting far less, than the buried evidence actually revealed. Refuse, it seemed, was a more reliable witness than memory or self-report, partly because few people ever imagine that a stranger will one day sift through their bins, and so they behave with unusual honesty. The bin, unlike the questionnaire, has no reason whatsoever to flatter its owner.

¶6 Recovering this evidence demands painstaking method. Excavated soil is passed through fine sieves to catch the small bones and beads that the naked eye would miss, and a technique called flotation, in which soil is gently washed in water, separates light fragments such as charred seeds that float up to the surface. In the laboratory, chemical analysis of the residues left inside a pot can reveal what it once held, while the state of the surrounding soil largely determines how much survives at all. Waterlogged and very dry sites are especially prized, since the absence of oxygen or of moisture can preserve fragile materials, such as wood and textile, that would simply vanish elsewhere. In such favourable conditions, objects thrown away thousands of years ago can emerge looking almost as fresh as the day they were first discarded.

¶7 There is a certain irony in all this for our own age. Future archaeologists studying the early twenty-first century will not lack for evidence, since we generate more waste than any people in history, much of it made of plastics that may endure for many centuries to come. What they make of us will depend on what survives, and a throwaway culture may, quite against its own intentions, leave behind the most detailed record any civilisation has ever produced. The humble rubbish heap, so easily overlooked today, may yet prove to be the most candid autobiography a whole society can write. Whether that is a comforting thought or an unsettling one is left, rather fittingly, for the future to decide.

options: Which remains last, and what they tell us
A heap that works like a calendar
Waste that contradicts the official story
Turning the method on the present day
The treasure that first drew explorers
A method too slow to be useful

questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says rubbish heaps are 'known to archaeologists as middens'.

questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 names the technique 'stratigraphy'.

sections.title: Passage 3 — Living with Uncertainty in Science
sections.instructions: You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 27–40.
sections.passage:
Living with Uncertainty in Science

¶1 The public often expects science to deliver certainty. A study is reported as having proved a claim; a new treatment is presented as a guaranteed cure; a forecast is judged a failure the moment reality departs from it by even a little. Scientists themselves, however, rarely speak in such absolute terms. For them, knowledge is provisional, always open to revision in the light of fresh evidence, and even the most firmly established theories are held with a degree of caution that can baffle outsiders. This gap between what the public wants from science and what science can honestly offer lies behind a great many of the misunderstandings that strain the relationship between them. Each side, in a sense, is disappointed by the other: the public by science's refusal to promise, and scientists by the public's reluctance to accept anything that falls short of a promise.

¶2 Part of the difficulty is that uncertainty, in science, does not mean simple ignorance. When a researcher reports that a measurement carries a margin of error, or that a result holds with a stated probability, this is not a confession of failure but a precise statement of how much confidence the evidence will support. Far from undermining a finding, such qualifications are a mark of its rigour. A claim presented with no uncertainty whatsoever attached to it should arouse suspicion rather than trust, for it suggests that the difficult work of measuring one's own doubt has simply not been done. Quantifying what one does not know is among the hardest and most valuable tasks in any science. It is also, unfortunately, the part of a result most likely to be stripped away before the finding ever reaches a general audience.

¶3 Communicating all this to a wider audience is notoriously hard. News media, working to tight deadlines and competing for attention, tend to prefer a clear story to an accurate one, and a hedged, probabilistic statement makes for a poor headline. The phrase scientists say, attached to a single study, can give the impression of a settled consensus where in truth there is lively debate, or can present a tentative early result as though it were a final verdict. When a later study reaches a different conclusion, as the ordinary process of science positively demands, the public may feel that it has been misled, and conclude that the experts simply cannot make up their minds. The fault, in such cases, lies less with the science than with a format that has no comfortable place for the words probably and so far.

¶4 How best to bridge this gap is itself a matter of dispute. Dr Lena Fischer argues that scientists should resist the temptation to oversimplify, and should instead teach the public to think in terms of probability and evidence, trusting people to handle complexity when it is explained to them with care. Professor Hassan Adib takes a more pragmatic line. He contends that, in a crowded and noisy information landscape, an honest message that is too complicated to be understood will simply be ignored, or displaced by a confident falsehood, and that clarity must sometimes take priority over completeness. Their disagreement is less about honesty than about how much nuance a distracted audience can realistically absorb. Both, it is worth noting, begin from the same commitment to telling the truth; they differ only over how that truth can best be made to survive the journey to a busy reader.

¶5 The stakes are raised sharply by those who exploit uncertainty on purpose. History offers several examples of industries which, faced with inconvenient findings, set out not to disprove the science but merely to amplify doubt, knowing that a public convinced the matter was unsettled would tolerate years of delay. By funding fringe studies and demanding ever more proof, such campaigns can manufacture the appearance of controversy long after researchers themselves have reached broad agreement. The ordinary and healthy uncertainty of science, in these hands, is twisted into a weapon against action, which is exactly why the careful communication of what is, and is not, genuinely in doubt matters far beyond the laboratory. A society that cannot tell the two apart is easily paralysed, talked into waiting for a certainty that is being withheld from it on purpose.

¶6 Meanwhile, decisions cannot always wait for a certainty that may never arrive. Governments must frequently act on incomplete evidence, weighing the cost of moving too soon against the cost of moving too late. In many situations the responsible course is to take precautions against a serious risk even before it has been proven beyond all doubt, much as a sensible person insures a house that is unlikely ever to burn down. Treating not yet certain as though it meant not real is itself a choice, and often a dangerous one, since some harms, once they have actually occurred, cannot be undone however conclusive the eventual proof turns out to be. Waiting, in other words, is never a neutral act; it is itself a decision, with consequences of its own.

¶7 The deeper lesson is that uncertainty is not a weakness of science to be apologised for, but the very engine of its progress. A discipline that openly admits what it does not know, and states how confidently it knows the rest, is in the end far more trustworthy than one that promises a certainty it cannot actually deliver. Teaching people to live comfortably with provisional knowledge, to act sensibly without waiting for perfect proof, and to tell honest doubt apart from manufactured confusion may be among the most important aims of education in a scientific age. Certainty, after all, is rare and precious; the ability to reason well in its absence is something that very nearly everyone turns out to need. In that sense, learning to live with uncertainty is not only a scientific skill but a thoroughly civic one.

questions.text: The public tends to expect certainty from science.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 opens by stating 'The public often expects science to deliver certainty.'

evidence.quote: The public often expects science to deliver certainty

questions.text: Scientists usually describe their knowledge as final and unchangeable.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 1 says that for scientists 'knowledge is provisional, always open to revision', contradicting 'final and unchangeable'.

evidence.quote: knowledge is provisional, always open to revision

questions.text: In science, uncertainty is simply another word for ignorance.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 states that uncertainty 'does not mean simple ignorance', so equating the two is wrong.

evidence.quote: does not mean simple ignorance

questions.text: A claim presented with no uncertainty attached deserves greater trust.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 2 says such a claim 'should arouse suspicion rather than trust', the opposite of deserving greater trust.

evidence.quote: should arouse suspicion rather than trust

questions.text: More scientists appear in the news media today than in the past.
questions.explanation: The passage discusses how media report science but never states whether more scientists appear now than before.

questions.text: The writer regards uncertainty as a strength of science rather than a flaw.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 7 says uncertainty is 'not a weakness of science to be apologised for, but the very engine of its progress'.

evidence.quote: not a weakness of science to be apologised for, but the very engine of its progress

questions.text: Why do the news media often prefer a clear story to an accurate one?
options: they work to tight deadlines and compete for attention

evidence.quote: working to tight deadlines and competing for attention

questions.text: Attaching 'scientists say' to a single study can wrongly suggest
questions.explanation: Paragraph 3 says the phrase can 'give the impression of a settled consensus' where debate actually exists.

evidence.quote: give the impression of a settled consensus

questions.text: Industries that exploit uncertainty deliberately aim to
questions.explanation: Paragraph 5 says they set out 'not to disprove the science but merely to amplify doubt'.

questions.text: An honest message that is too complicated will simply be ignored.
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4: Adib contends 'an honest message that is too complicated to be understood will simply be ignored'.

questions.text: Clarity must sometimes take priority over completeness.
